coredata.dc:title: Transforming bio-waste lignin into amine functionalized carbon quantum dots for selective detection of trace Cu2+ in aqueous system
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Haitao
Chen, Yuqing
Labidi, Abdelkader
Zhao, Ke
Xu, Xiaoqian
Othman, Sarah I.
Allam, Ahmed A.
Rudayni, Hassan Ahmad
Wang, Chuanyi
coredata.dc:description: Developing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from bio-waste lignin for effectively detecting Cu2+ is of great significance for promoting the value-added utilization of lignin resources. However, the limited amount of surface-active groups and low quantum yield of lignin-based CQDs hinder their application in this regard. Herein, bio-waste lignin was converted into value-added amine functionalized CQDs using a facile two-step hydrothermal approach. The as-synthesized CQDs modified with amino groups exhibit bright green fluorescence, abundant surface functional groups, high water solubility and uniform particle size (3.9 nm). Systematic analysis demonstrates that the rich NH2 groups (~12.3 %) on the CQDs backbone improve their fluorescence properties (quantum yield increased from 3.4 % to 21.1 %) and specific detection ability for Cu2+. The developed NH2-CQDs serve as an efficient fluorescent probe, displaying high sensitivity and selectivity towards Cu2+ in aqueous system, with a detection limit of 2.42 μmol/L, which is lower than the maximum permitted amount of Cu2+ in drinking water (20 μmol/L). The detection mechanism of NH2-CQDs for Cu2+ is attributed to the synergy of static quenching and photo-induced electron transfer. This study provides a valuable reference for the synthesis of high-quality fluorescent CQDs from lignin resources and the effective detection of trace Cu2+ in aquatic environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin-based carbon quantum dots
Surface functionalization
Fluorescent sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sustainable production of CQDs and NH2-CQDs
Detection of Cu2+ by the NH2-CQDs
Determination mechanisms of Cu2+ by NH2-CQDs

The authors gratefully recognize the Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project (PNURSP2024R5), Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sustainable synthesis of NH2-CQDs from bio-waste lignin and their application for trace Cu2+ detection.
(a, b and c) TEM images of NH2-CQDs at different resolutions, as well as (d) size distribution histogram.
(a) XPS survey spectra of CQDs and NH2-CQDs, (b) CQDs C1s, (c) CQDs O1s, (d) NH2-CQDs N1s, (e) NH2-CQDs C1s and (f) NH2-CQDs O1s.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) zeta potentials, and (c) UV–vis spectra of CQDs and NH2-CQDs. (d) CIE coordinates of NH2-CQDs.

(a) The PL spectra of NH2-CQDs at diverse excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 to 490 nm. (b) 3D fluorescence topographical map of NH2-CQDs. (c) PL stability of NH2-CQDs under diverse pH conditions and (d) different NaCl concentrations (λ ex = 412 nm).

(a) The influence of varying incubation times on the PL intensity of NH2-CQDs after the introduction of 200 μmol/L Cu2+. (b) PL intensity of NH2-CQDs in the existence of diverse ions (200 μmol/L). (c) PL spectra of NH2-CQDs after adding diverse concentrations of Cu2+. (d) Linear relationships between F/F 0 and Cu2+ in the range of 0.5–80 μmol/L.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of Cu2+ and the excitation and emission spectra of NH2-CQDs. (b) PL lifetimes of NH2-CQDs and NH2-CQDs+Cu2+ (80 μmol/L). (c) The effect of adding diverse concentrations of Cu2+ on the UV–vis spectra of NH2-CQDs. (d) Schematic mechanism diagram of the static quenching process between NH2-CQDs and Cu2+.
(a) The CV curve of NH2-CQDs. (b) Tauc plot [(αhν)2 versus hν] of NH2-CQDs suspension obtained from the UV–vis spectrum. (c) Possible mechanism between Cu2+ and NH2-CQDs.
Comparative analysis of several CQDs used to detect Cu2+.

head.ce:title: Transforming bio-waste lignin into amine functionalized carbon quantum dots for selective detection of trace Cu2+ in aqueous system

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Haitao Ren and Yuqing Chen contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from bio-waste lignin for effectively detecting Cu2+ is of great significance for promoting the value-added utilization of lignin resources. However, the limited amount of surface-active groups and low quantum yield of lignin-based CQDs hinder their application in this regard. Herein, bio-waste lignin was converted into value-added amine functionalized CQDs using a facile two-step hydrothermal approach. The as-synthesized CQDs modified with amino groups exhibit bright green fluorescence, abundant surface functional groups, high water solubility and uniform particle size (3.9 nm). Systematic analysis demonstrates that the rich NH2 groups (~12.3 %) on the CQDs backbone improve their fluorescence properties (quantum yield increased from 3.4 % to 21.1 %) and specific detection ability for Cu2+. The developed NH2-CQDs serve as an efficient fluorescent probe, displaying high sensitivity and selectivity towards Cu2+ in aqueous system, with a detection limit of 2.42 μmol/L, which is lower than the maximum permitted amount of Cu2+ in drinking water (20 μmol/L). The detection mechanism of NH2-CQDs for Cu2+ is attributed to the synergy of static quenching and photo-induced electron transfer. This study provides a valuable reference for the synthesis of high-quality fluorescent CQDs from lignin resources and the effective detection of trace Cu2+ in aquatic environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: According to reports, plant growth produces around 150 billion tons of lignin yearly, with roughly 50 million tons of industrial lignin removed from the pulping and biorefining sectors [1]. However, >95 % of industrial lignin is not utilized reasonably and effectively, and the majority of the lignin is burned as low-grade solid fuel or directly released into rivers and lakes as black liquor [2,3].

These facts overlook the potential commercial value of lignin waste and cause substantial environmental damage and potential resource waste. In fact, lignin is the most abundant aromatic natural macromolecular compound on earth, containing oxygen groups (i.e., methoxy, hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, etc.) with the advantages of its easy surface functionalization and structural modification [4–6]. CQDs represent a novel class of carbon-based fluorescent materials, which has excellent biocompatibility, good hydrophilicity, high stability, low toxicity and adjustable optical properties [7–10]. Owing to these merits, CQDs have found widespread applications in diverse fields such as biological imaging, environmental monitoring, anti-counterfeiting printing, photocatalysis, drug sensing and food packaging [11–16]. Bio-waste lignin is employed as a precursor, which has significant advantages in the production of functional carbon nanomaterials like CQDs. Because itself not only provides a suitable carbon source, but also minimizes the use of toxic or expensive chemicals.

In recent years, different types of lignin biomass (i.e., alkali lignin [17,18], enzymatic hydrolysis lignin [19], G-, S-, and H-type lignin [20], sodium lignosulfonate [21], and enzymatic kraft lignin [22]) have been utilized as precursors for hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs to realize its value-added utilization and decrease CQDs cost. However, the quantum yield (QY) of CQDs derived directly from lignin via hydrothermal treatment is low, followed by a limited amount of surface-active groups, which limits their wide practical applications. In this regard, it is desired to modify lignin-based CQDs to further expand their potential applications. Nitrogen modification is a prevalent strategy for regulating the structure of CQDs and improving their photoluminescence (PL) performance [23,24]. Table S1 summarizes the properties of N-modified CQDs (N-CQDs) developed in recent years. It can be observed that most N-CQDs were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method, resulting in limited exposure of active groups on the surface of CQDs with poor optical properties (QY typically below 18 %). These shortages hinder the application of lignin-based CQDs in heavy metal detection. Therefore, it is necessary to explore an effective approach for preparing lignin-based CQDs with high QY and high surface-active groups.

One of the essential metal ions, Cu2+, is employed as a micronutrient for people, soil, and plants [25]. Through the enrichment of the food chain, Cu2+ can reach the human body. Nevertheless, an excessive buildup of Cu2+ in the human body poses a significant risk to human health and the natural environment [26]. Additionally, the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) has set regulations for drinking water that have a higher and safer content limit of Cu2+ at 20 μmol/L [27]. So far, as environmental and human health concerns, the detection of Cu2+ is vitally crucial. In recent years, fluorescent probe approaches based on CQDs have attracted significant attention in the field of environmental pollution detection [28,29], because of their simple operation, easy synthesis of probes, quick response times, and low cost. Therefore, developing more CQD-based fluorescent probes with high sensitivity and selectivity is of great significance for promoting the detection of heavy metals in water environments.

In the present work, the bio-waste lignin was transformed into value-added amine functionalized CQDs (NH2-CQDs) for detecting trace Cu2+ in the aqueous medium, via a simple two-step hydrothermal process (Fig. 1 ). Interestingly, by precisely modifying the surface of CQDs derived from direct hydrothermal treatment of lignin with polyetherimide (PEI), the obtained NH2-CQDs exhibit a high QY of 21.1 % at the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 412 and 512 nm, respectively, which is better than most reported lignin-based CQDs (Table S1). Besides, surface modification of NH2 groups significantly enhances the recognition ability of CQDs for Cu2+. As a result, the developed NH2-CQDs as a fluorescence probe exhibit great selectivity and sensitivity towards trace Cu2+ with a fast response time (~1 min) and low detection limit of 2.42 μmol/L. Furthermore, the detection mechanism of NH2-CQDs towards trace Cu2+ is elucidated based on systematic analysis by combining various characterization approaches.

Alkali lignin and polyetherimide (M.W. 1800) were procured from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). BaCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, KCl, AgNO3, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, PbSO4, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Zr(NO3)4·5H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Na2CO3, La(NO3)3·6H2O, Sr(NO3)2, and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were procured from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The filter membranes with a pore diameter of 50 nm were obtained from Haining Yibo Filter Equipment Company (Zhejiang, China). Dialysis bags with a molecular weight cut-off of 2500 Da were manufactured by Yibo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hunan, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sustainable production of CQDs and NH2-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: First, 0.3 g lignin was mixed with 30 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 2 h. Afterward, the mixture was sealed in a 50 mL hydrothermal autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 16 h. After the reaction, the solution was filtered several times with a 50 nm filter membrane, the obtained brown-yellow solution was dried at 80 °C for 12 h to collect CQDs powder. Furthermore, 100 mg of the above-dried CQDs were mingled with 30 mL water and different doses of PEI (100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μL). Then, the CQDs mixtures were placed into a 50 mL autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling to 25 °C, reacted solutions were filtered through a 50 nm microporous membrane. The collected yellowish-brown suspension was dialyzed with a 2500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to detach residual PEI and water was replaced every 6 h, after which the liquid in the bag was saved. Finally, NH2-CQDs powder was obtained after drying for 12 h at 80 °C.

400 μL of PEI was mixed with 30 mL of ultrapure water. After stirring for 1 h, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the collected pale-yellow liquid was dialyzed with a 2500 D dialysis bag for 24 h to separate the residual PEI, and the water was changed every 6 h. Finally, the aqueous solution of CQDs derived from PEI was obtained, which was named PEI-CQDs.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-F200 microscope, operating at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were determined on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer with a 250 W Al K X-ray excitation source. The zeta potential of samples was tested using Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were determined using a Brucker (Verte70 V) equipment in the KBr medium. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured employing a Shimadzu UV 2600 spectrophotometer. An Edinburgh Instruments FS5 spectrofluorometer was used to measure the PL spectra, and the same instrument was used to estimate PL lifetimes using an EPLED-340 pulsed source.

To analyze the LUMO energy level of CQDs, the cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed using an IGS4030 electrochemical workstation (Guangzhou, China) with a three-electrode system. The following was the working electrode preparation: A solution containing 0.5 mg/mL NH2-CQDs in ethanol was produced, and 1 mL of the suspension was dropped over an ITO conductive glass surface. After leaving it to dry naturally, the constructed working electrode, then together with the other two electrodes (Ag/AgCl electrode and platinum electrode), was placed in an electrolytic cell containing 0.2 mol/L Na2SO4 solution. Using a voltage range of −1.0 to 1.1 V, the CV curve was created at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

Different types of metal ions were employed for the selectivity test, with a concentration of 200 μmol/L, including Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Ag+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Na+, Zn2+, Zr4+, Mn2+, Fe2+, La3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Sr2+, Hg2+, and Cu2+. The solution of NH2-CQDs (1 mL, 400 μg/mL) was mixed with these metal ions, accordingly. Moreover, the sensitivity of NH2-CQDs for detecting Cu2+ was investigated by combining NH2-CQDs solution (1 mL, 400 μg/mL) with Cu2+ concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μmol/L. The PL spectra of an NH2-CQDs solution containing Cu2+ stimulated at 412 nm were measured after 5 min at room temperature. Ultrapure water that was at room temperature was used to prepare all samples.

TEM was applied to elucidate the microscopic morphology and particle diameter of the CQDs derived from lignin waste. The produced NH2-CQDs are distributed without any aggregation in a spherical-like shape, as shown by the TEM image in Fig. 2a–b. Moreover, the NH2-CQDs are well crystallized, as seen by the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2c), which equates to (100) planes of graphite with a lattice spacing of 0.214 nm [30]. Fig. 2d displays the histogram of the NH2-CQDs particle size distribution obtained using the software Nano Measure 1.2. The histogram reveals that the size distribution of NH2-CQDs particles ranged from 2.5 to 7 nm, with a mean particle size of 3.9 nm. The particle size for NH2-CQDs is <10 nm indicating that the sample size is in the quantum dot range.

XPS technology was performed to interpret the chemical bond changes between CQDs and NH2-CQDs. The XPS survey spectra include C1s (285.0 eV) and O1s (531.7 eV) peaks for CQDs and NH2-CQDs, as well as an additional N1s peak (399 eV) for NH2-CQDs (Fig. 3a). The NH2-CQDs have a high surface N content of 12.3 %, confirming the successful modification of amine groups. Fig. 3b–e shows that the high-resolution C1s spectra of CQDs and NH2-CQDs exhibit three similar peaks at 284.8, 285.7, and 287.6 eV, corresponding to the C-C/C=C, CO, and CO bonds [31], respectively. It is worth noting that a new CN bond appears at 286.4 eV for NH2-CQDs, indicating the bonding of -NH2 in PEI molecules to -COOH on the surface of CQDs. Fig. 3c–f presents the high-resolution O1s spectra to explain the bond configurations of O atoms in the CQDs and NH2-CQDs. The O1s spectra are divided into two components at 531.2 and 532.5 eV, which are ascribed to CO and CO bonds, respectively [32]. As demonstrated in Fig. 3d, the N1s spectrum is deconvoluted into three components centered at 399.2, 400.2, and 401.0 eV reflecting the bonding structure of C-N-C or pyridine N, N-(C)3, and NH bonds respectively, which confirms that the CQDs are successfully functionalized by PEI [33,34]. XPS spectra analysis indicates that abundant functional groups (-OH, -NH2 and -CONH2) are present at the surface of the NH2-CQDs, which greatly enhance their water solubility and stability. This result is further verified by the FT-IR spectrum. The FT-IR spectrum of NH2-CQDs (Fig. 4a) shows absorption bands at wavenumbers of 3594, 1573, and 1460 cm−1, which correspond to the stretch vibrations of N-H/O-H, C=C-N, and CN, respectively. Besides, the wavenumbers at 2918, 1670, and 1040 cm−1 are associated with CH, CO, and CO stretching vibration bands, respectively [35,36]. These characteristic peaks of NH2-CQDs are significantly enhanced compared to the CQDs without NH2 functionalization.

Moreover, the zeta potential of CQDs suspension is −25.6 mV (Fig. 4b), implying that the oxygen-containing groups (e.g., -OH, -COOH) are located on the surface of CQDs. However, the zeta potential of NH2-CQDs suspension is only −2.22 mV, which may be the result of an interaction between the carboxyl group on the surface of CQDs and NH2 in PEI during hydrothermal reaction [37]. This result further demonstrates that PEI molecules were covalently combined on the surface of CQDs during hydrothermal reaction. From XPS, FTIR and zeta potential analysis, it can be concluded that compared with pure CQDs, the NH2-CQDs have more abundant N-containing functional groups on their surface, which would be conducive to the specific recognition of Cu2+ by NH2-CQDs.

Firstly, the influence of the quantity of PEI on the PL properties of the synthesized NH2-CQDs was optimized. As revealed in Fig. S1, the PL intensity of NH2-CQDs at an optimum excitation wavelength of 412 nm first increases and then decreases with increasing the dosage of PEI from 0 to 500 μL. Among them, when the added amount of PEI is 400 μL, the PL intensity of NH2-CQDs reaches its maximum. This may be due to an appropriate amount of PEI molecules reacting with functional groups on the surface of CQDs, producing abundant N-containing surface states which led to enhanced QY [38]. Thus, the optimized dosage of PEI is 400 μL during the synthesis of NH2-CQDs. Furthermore, the optimal excitation and emission spectra of PEI-CQDs are compared with CQDs and NH2-CQDs. As shown in Fig. S2, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of PEI-CQDs are 344 and 467 nm, while emitting blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for CQDs and NH2-CQDs are about 412 and 512 nm, and NH2-CQDs emit stronger green fluorescence than CQDs under 365 nm UV irradiation. These results indicate that PEI has successfully modified the CQDs and enhanced their fluorescence performance.

The optical behaviors of NH2-CQDs were studied utilizing UV–vis absorption spectra. As exhibited in Fig. 4c, a strong peak at 216 nm is ascribed to the π-π* transition of CC bands, and another shoulder peak at 281 nm is ascribed to the n-π* transition of CO bands [39]. After CQDs modification by PEI, the absorption spectrum of CQDs shows a redshift and appears an extra absorption peak at 345 nm. The phenomenon is associated with the n-π* transition of C=O/C=N bands, indicating the occurrence of an amidation reaction on the surface of CQDs [40]. The color of the NH2-CQDs suspension is transparent yellow-brown under sunlight, the bright green fluorescence can be seen visually when the NH2-CQDs suspension was irradiated under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 4c, inset). In Fig. 4d, the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) coordinates at (0.3123, 0.4896) acquired from emission results at 412 nm excitation were used to further validate the NH2-CQDs suspension green fluorescence. The PL spectra of NH2-CQDs under various excitation wavelengths were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 5a, when the excitation wavelength gradually changes from 340 to 490 nm, the intensity of the emission peak first increases and then decreases, and the emission center exhibits an obvious redshift, implying that the NH2-CQDs have excitation-dependent characteristics. This phenomenon was further demonstrated by normalized PL spectra (Fig. S3). These distinctive PL properties of NH2-CQDs can be ascribed to the various defect states generated by surface oxidation and NH2 modification. In the 3D fluorescence spectra of NH2-CQDs, a single emission center comparable to concentric rings was observed (Fig. 5b), showing that the material is a homogenous substance. Furthermore, the absolute PL QY of NH2-CQDs was determined to be 21.1 % using an integrating sphere (Fig. S4), which is significantly higher than that of CQDs (3.4 %) obtained from direct hydrothermal processing of lignin. This result indicates that the surface functionalization of NH2 significantly improves the QY of CQD, which may be due to the modification of NH2 increasing the surface defect states of NH2-CQDs. In addition, the QY of the designed NH2-CQDs is relatively higher than that of most reported N-modified or unmodified lignin-based CQDs (Table S1). More importantly, the surface of NH2-CQDs contains rich amino functional groups, which greatly promotes the application of NH2-CQDs in heavy metal detection.

The impact of various factors, such as pH and ionic strength, was examined to investigate the PL stability of the produced NH2-CQDs. Fig. 5c shows that the NH2-CQDs have steady PL properties in the pH range of 5 to 10. The PL intensity of NH2-CQDs decreased when the solution is strongly acidic (pH < 5) or alkaline (pH > 10). This phenomenon may be explained by the protonation or deprotonation of surface groups like -COOH and -NH2 under harsh pH conditions [41]. Therefore, the feasible property in a pH range of 5.0–10.0 makes NH2-CQDs ideal candidates for ion detection. Additionally, under NaCl concentrations up to 1.2 mol/L, there is no discernible attenuation in PL intensity (Fig. 5d). These results evidence that as-produced NH2-CQDs show stable PL characteristics, and they hold considerable promise for real-world applications such as environmental detection under complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ by the NH2-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To compare the detection potential of CQDs and NH2-CQDs for Cu2+, their fluorescence spectra after adding Cu2+ were measured under the same conditions. As shown in Figs. S5 and 6 b, after adding 200 μmol/L of Cu2+ ions, CQDs maintained 72 % of fluorescence intensity, while NH2-CQDs only maintained 38 % of fluorescence intensity. This indicates that NH2-CQDs have a greater detection ability for Cu2+ compared to CQDs, which may be due to the enhanced coordination between CQDs and Cu2+ by the introduction of NH2 groups. Besides, the obtained lignin-based NH2-CQDs exhibit favorable hydrophilicity, abundant surface functional groups, high fluorescence QY, and great fluorescent stability. These features suggest that NH2-CQDs are an excellent probe for identifying Cu2+ in aqueous solutions, allowing us to evaluate their detection potential for Cu2+.

First, the PL quenching efficiency (F/F 0) of NH2-CQDs is highly dependent on the culture duration. Six-time intervals were utilized in this case to assess the ideal incubation period. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fast reaction of Cu2+ detection by NH2-CQDs was verified by the PL quenching time experiment. After adding Cu2+, the PL intensity of NH2-CQDs is quickly quenched to a steady low level in less than a minute, demonstrating the ability of NH2-CQDs to respond quickly in Cu2+ detection. Furthermore, the selectivity of NH2-CQDs for the detection of Cu2+ was investigated. The effects of several metal ions (i.e., Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Ag+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Na+, Zn2+, Zr4+, Mn2+, Fe2+, La3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Sr2+, Hg2+, and Cu2+) on the PL intensity of NH2-CQDs at 412 nm excitation wavelength were analyzed. Following the introduction of various metal ions, the histogram in Fig. 6b shows a variation in the PL intensities (F/F 0, where F 0 is the PL intensity before metal mixing) of NH2-CQDs. The addition of Cu2+ significantly reduces the PL intensity of the NH2-CQDs, while other metal ions have a minor effect. This phenomenon demonstrates a high selectivity of NH2-CQDs for the detection of Cu2+, suggesting great potential for Cu2+ identification in aqueous environments.

To inspect the sensitivity of NH2-CQDs for detecting Cu2+, the PL spectra of the NH2-CQDs was examined by adding Cu2+ solution at various concentrations varying between 0 and 200 μmol/L. As shown in Fig. 6c, when the concentration of Cu2+ increases (from 0 to 200 μmol/L), the PL intensity of NH2-CQDs attenuates continuously. When Cu2+ was added to NH2-CQDs solution and exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp, a significant reduction in green fluorescence in the visual domain was observed (Fig. S6). Moreover, Fig. 6d shows a strong linear connection (R 2 = 0.9961) when the concentration of Cu2+ is between 0.5 and 80 μmol/L. The fluorescence change can be followed with the equation:
F 0
F
= 1 + K sv
Q
where [Q] denotes the Cu2+ concentration, Ksv stands for the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and F and F 0 are the PL intensities of NH2-CQDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 2.42 μmol/L using the 3δ/s rule [42,43], where δ represents the standard deviation and s denotes the standard curve slope. This value is significantly lower than the Cu2+ content limit of 20 μmol/L set by the USEPA in drinking water. This outcome suggests that the NH2-CQDs can be applied to determine the water environment's quality in terms of Cu2+ content. Moreover, Table 1 generalizes the detection performance of CQDs generated using various approaches for Cu2+ detection. Compared with other CQDs for detecting Cu2+, the NH2-CQDs in the current study exhibit comparable or even greater potential for Cu2+ detection due to their advantages of relatively low LOD, rapid response and simple feedstock preparation. However, the recoverability of these CQDs-based probes for Cu2+ detection is unclear, and future studies on the recyclability of these probes need to be strengthened.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination mechanisms of Cu2+ by NH2-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the mechanism of PL quenching induced by Cu2+ in NH2-CQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra and PL lifetimes of NH2-CQDs after the addition of diverse concentrations of Cu2+ were measured in detail. The UV–vis spectrum of the Cu2+ solution shows two wide absorption peaks at 226 and 302 nm (Fig. 7a). The generated NH2-CQDs with a PL excitation wavelength of 412 nm and an emission wavelength of 512 nm do not overlap with the absorption peaks of Cu2+ ions throughout the sensing process. It can be deduced that the PL attenuation of NH2-CQDs by Cu2+ can't be caused by the internal filtering effect between NH2-CQDs and Cu2+ [51]. The PL lifetimes of NH2-CQDs after adding Cu2+ were investigated to further understand the detection mechanism of Cu2+ by NH2-CQDs. Fig. 7b shows that the fluorescence decay curves can be fitted with the double exponential function (Table S2). The almost identical lifetime values indicate that the PL lifetime of NH2-CQD is not affected by the addition of Cu2+. This result implies that the formation of a non-fluorescent NH2-CQDs/Cu2+ complex may be the source of static quenching, which is the sensing mechanism of NH2-CQDs for Cu2+. Furthermore, when the concentration of Cu2+ increases, the absorption peak of NH2-CQDs at 272 nm is progressively red-shifted and strengthened (Fig. 7c). The changes in the absorption spectra of NH2-CQDs again indicate that PL quenching is caused by static quenching [52,53]. According to the results mentioned above, the PL quenching behavior of NH2-CQDs is primarily ascribed to static quenching, where the surface groups of NH2-CQDs can affiliate Cu2+ and generate ground state complexes, resulting in effective fluorescent NH2-CQDs become less (Fig. 7d).

To determine whether PL quenching was achieved via photo-induced electron transfer (PET), the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of NH2-CQDs were examined. According to the CV curve (Fig. 8a), the reduction potential (Ered) of NH2-CQDs is −0.39 V. Then, the ELUMO of NH2-CQDs was assessed using the formula:
E LUMO
= − e
E red
+ 4.4
and found to be −4.01 eV [54]. Based on the Tauc plot and UV–vis spectra, the band gap (Eg) of NH2-CQDs was determined to be 3.14 eV (Fig. 8b). Following that, the EHOMO of NH2-CQDs was calculated to be −7.15 eV using the equation:
E HOMO
=
E LUMO
−
E g
. Cu2+ has an electrode potential of −4.74 eV [55], which is higher than the HOMO energy level but lower than the LUMO energy level of NH2-CQDs. Moreover, the chelation between the N-containing groups on the surface of NH2-CQDs and Cu2+ ions is conducive to the occurrence of charge transfer. As a result, excited electrons on the ELUMO of NH2-CQDs have a thermodynamic tendency to migrate to the ELUMO of Cu2+ (Fig. 8c). This migration inhibits the electron radiation transition, which leads to the PL quenching of NH2-CQDs. Ultimately, the synergistic effect of PET and static quenching is responsible for the detection mechanism of present NH2-CQDs towards Cu2+.

In summary, NH2-CQDs were synthesized from bio-waste lignin by employing a facile two-step hydrothermal strategy, thereby transforming waste into a valuable product while reducing environmental pollution. The produced NH2-CQDs exhibit a particle size of 3.9 nm, robust stability, and bright green fluorescence with a QY of 21.1 %, a remarkable improvement from the 3.4 % QY of unmodified CQDs. Additionally, the N-containing functional groups on the NH2-CQDs backbone enhance its hydrophilicity and detection ability of trace Cu2+. Based on the synergistic effect of photo-induced electron transfer and static quenching, the developed NH2-CQDs demonstrated exceptional selectivity and sensitivity towards trace Cu2+ with a low LOD of 2.42 μmol/L and rapid response time (~1 min). The LOD is lower than the maximum permitted Cu2+ concentration in drinking water (20 μmol/L), indicating that NH2-CQDs have significant potential to detect Cu2+ for water quality assessment. This work gives a sustainable and reliable approach for the value-added utilization of lignin resources and augments the application of CQDs in environment.

Haitao Ren: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Yuqing Chen: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Abdelkader Labidi: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Ke Zhao: Investigation. Xiaoqian Xu: Validation. Sarah I. Othman: Methodology. Ahmed A. Allam: Formal analysis. Hassan Ahmad Rudayni: Resources. Chuanyi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52161145409), and SAFEA of China (“Belt and Road” Innovative Exchange Foreign Expert Project, DL2023041004L). The authors gratefully recognize the Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project (PNURSP2024R5), Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, C. Zhou, S. Wu, C. Sun, Ion-imprinted polymer modified with carbon quantum dots as a highly sensitive copper (II) ion probe, Polymers 13 (2021) 1376, doi:10.3390/polym13091376.

Chen, Nitrogen-doped fluorescence carbon dots as multi-mechanism detection for iodide and curcumin in biological and food samples, Bioact. Mater. 6 (2021) 1541, doi:10.1016/j.bioactmat.2020.11.006.
